data.title: Is Your Retirement Income Enough? How I Leveled Up Mine With Covered Call ETFs
data.description:
The harsh reality is that most asset prices are negatively correlated with the changes in interest rates. For example, if the rates go down, the asset prices go up. The mathematical consequence of this is that the yields tend to fall as the denominator in the 'dividend per share to share price' formula increases.

Given that the Fed has already made several cuts from the peak interest rate level in mid-2024, we should not be surprised that the overall yield levels for most assets have gone down accordingly. Be it T-bills (TBIL), real estate (VNQ), high-yield bonds (HYG), infrastructure (BIP), telecommunication (IYZ) etc., almost everywhere we look, the yields are indeed lower than, say, at the start of 2024.

When it comes to the immediate future, then there is nothing positive to say in relation to enjoying attractive yields. Namely, the chances are very high that soon (December 2025 or early 2026) the Fed would announce incremental base rate cuts, putting an additional pressure on the asset yields.

All of this is not good news for investors, who have the following objectives:

  • Retire or live off from passive income.
  • Grow the income-producing asset base to hit the envisaged income figure.
  • Take balanced risk, which would reduce income volatility and, in general, warrant better sleep at night.
  • Avoid portfolio asset sales to fund consumption needs.

In other words, investors, who are focused on building up a significant passive income machine without taking too aggressive risks have now (and will have going forward) a more difficult job at hand. And by difficult I mean achieving the necessary target income level by a certain date or in as capital efficient way as possible.

If I look deeper into my portfolio, then apart from BDCs (BIZD), MLPs (AMLP) and leveraged closed end funds (PDI), I have to say that the reinvestment yields have become much less attractive than before. While in early 2024 I could easily find assets that, on average, provided safe 7% to 9% yields, then now I have to say the amplitude has moved down into 5% to 7% territory.

For example, in just over the past 6 month period, British American Tobacco (BTI) yield has decreased by 21%, for Alpine Income Property Trust, Inc. (NYSE:PINE) by 9%, for Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG) by 6%, for Janus Henderson B-BBB CLO ETF (JBBB) by 6% etc. The only areas where the yields have somewhat increased in my portfolio are where I have already mentally factored in some income reductions in the next 1 - 3 year period - more specifically, the BDC and leveraged CEF space.

Long story short, the current situation for income investors is worse than it was not that long ago and going forward it will very likely become even more difficult (time consuming) to snowball passive income flows.

Now, luckily, we (and I) have the covered call ETF opportunity (segment) to explore and integrate in our income portfolios to fight back the yield-pressures coming from more traditional income-oriented assets.

The way how I approach covered call ETFs

If we think about the covered call ETF pay-off profiles, then I would say there are some fundamental similarities to high-yield credit.

Just think of it.

The pay-off for high-yield credit consists of two components:

  • Getting back principal or slightly profiting from its increase if, say, the high-yield issuer delivers robust financials, experiences positive credit migration etc.
  • Pocketing relatively attractive coupon, which usually explains the lion's share of total return that is associated with this high-yield credit investment.

At the same time, we we are assuming fully open downside risk where we could end up losing the entire principal (issuer default) and relatively risky coupons (could be delayed, reduced after potential restructuring etc.).

Now, if we look at a standard covered call ETF - JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) -, which writes out of the money call options on a certain underlying index, then basically the pay-off profile becomes quite similar to the high-yield case:

  • Potentially profiting from slight price appreciation that would come from the index increasing in price until the capped ceiling (from call option sale) is reached.
  • Capturing very attractive but inherently volatile dividends, which tend to also constitute majority of the total return figure.

Speaking of risks, there is also almost fully open downside risk that could materialize from plunging index prices, where the dividends in such case would also quickly disappear.

For this reason (bond-like pay-off profile) and the fact that I already have a decent capital deployed in PIMCO CEFs as well as BDCs, I have imposed a rather tight limit on the maximum covered call ETF exposure in my overall portfolio. The maximum is 15%, and, currently, the exposure is around 12%.

Having said that, since the average yield on my covered call ETF bucket is 13%, then still, I get a quite decent bang for the buck in the context of alternative ~7% yielding assets. To quickly put things into perspective, I would need 85% of additional capital into 7% yielding securities to arrive at the same level of income that my covered call ETFs generate.

However, I see that there are at least three important steps how we could optimize our covered call ETF exposures from the risk management and, ultimately, durable income generation perspective.

First, avoid covered call ETFs, which sell call options against a single company instead of a broadly diversified index or asset class, where the downside volatility is much less pronounced. YieldMax is quite active here with such vehicles as YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (TSLY) and YieldMax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (MSTY). Both of these funds have experienced significant NAV erosion and steep distribution cuts. Granted, if we add back the distributions made, the total return figures remain somewhat neutral (albeit, it depends on what time period we choose), but it does not change the fact that the risk level is too high for any prudent income investor.

Second, avoid building up overly concentrated covered call ETF positions in the typical indices - the S&P 500 (SPY) and the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ). This is not easy as the most well-know covered call ETF funds such as JEPQ, the Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX), the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI), the Neos S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI) are linked to exactly SPY or QQQ. The issue is that, at least in my view, these indices are overvalued and in the case of AI-driven correction would almost certainly plunge. So, the combination of that happening and having most of portfolio covered call ETFs tied to tech-exposed areas would not be the greatest event to experience.

Here, I see a significant merit of including covered call ETFs (on top of some allocation in the AI-linked names) that are based on less correlated indices. My personal top picks are Neos Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI) for real estate factor and the NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI) for gold factor. It is clear that in the case of armageddon event (system-wide sell-off), the asset price correlations converge to 1, thus weakening the argument or benefit of such diversification. However, not all sell-off are system-wide and, most likely, if the AI bet starts to lose steam, then I would easily see a case for the QQQ dropping at much larger rate than, say, real estate or gold.

Third, avoid building significant concentration into zero day option expiry covered call ETFs such as the Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (XDTE) and the Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (QDTE). While these funds generate 30%+ dividend yields, they come at an elevated risk of suffering from a permanent impairment of NAV in a scenario where the market turns deeply south in a single-day period. The issue is that they lock in gains (capped gains) or losses (100% downside adjusted for option premiums) that have occurred in a single day and then in the following day write new call option contracts against the updated underlying value. If the downside path for the market is gradual, then XDTE and QDTE could outperform. Conversely, if the market experiences a sharp correction within one trading day and then recovers in the next day, XDTE and QDTE would not be able to benefit from the recovery, and, instead, be left with structurally impaired value base from which the forthcoming dividends would be lower accordingly.

The bottom line

Covered call ETFs are powerful tools for portfolio yield enhancement and income diversification in a falling interest rate environment, where many traditional income sources become less and less attractive.

At the same time, we have to be cognizant of risks that come with these high-yielding covered call ETF structures; which is only logical given the double digit dividends.

The key is to keep the covered call ETF exposure balanced (even though I fully understand the tendency to really overweight these ETFs due to their attractive yields) and to calibrate covered call portfolio in a manner that maximizes risk and return factor diversification in a prudent manner.

data.title: AI Won't Kill Adobe, Here's Why
data.description:
The argument against Adobe

This is the second time I covered Adobe (NASDAQ:ADBE), the first time around was not successful with the shares dropping even further by -25%. This is one of those strange situations where the numbers continue to improve, yet Wall St. is betting that new technologies in artificial intelligence will eventually destroy Adobe's business or make similar services so cheap that price competition will ensue.

There's no doubt that new AI photo editing systems like Google's (GOOG)(GOOGL) nano banana, mid journey, Dall E and others will eventually have an easily accessible app and that individuals that do a lot of multi-media editing will discover where to find it. For now, Adobe's numbers speak for themselves. These enterprise software systems are sticky and hard for companies to detach from once they subscribe to the product and have an ongoing workflow that their workforce uses such apps for everyday.

While I personally agree that what Google, OpenAI and others have done with instant picture alterations is amazing, it is not all that easy to operate for those that are not used to working with LLM systems.

LLMs will be engines rather than re-create every platform

Anti trust laws exist for a reason and the companies with those killer AI apps like Google, Open AI and Anthropic, would be best served to simply offer their models as engines rather than try to eat everyone else's lunch. They can still monetize their products without much scrutiny in this fashion while still allowing users to perform similar functions to popular software services through their Swiss army knife like chat apps. The minute they begin to create more and more stand alone apps or acquire companies in SAAS spaces that already have users bases is a risk that might not be worth taking.

I brought up a similar idea when looking at Docusign (DOCU), which I also still believe is incredibly cheap. The company had just had a sell off because ChatGPT created an "agent" that could analyze legal contracts in similar fashion to Docusign. However, if you read through Docusign's last investor presentation, you also realize that they themselves are woking with OpenAI to introduce such functions into their already familiar user interface.

This is also the crux of the argument, individuals are already very accustomed to the UI's of an a Adobe or a Docusign. A user would in my opinion much rather have an improved experience [incorporating these technologies into the UI behind the scenes], rather than learn something new.

LLMs are not all that intuitive for many

For those that type well and express themselves quickly, LLMs are a dream come true. I've personally run into many individuals who either hate typing or have fear of communicating with artificial intelligence for personal reasons. They are used to clicking buttons or dragging and dropping versus asking for something via their own language and creativity. I still believe that the button/drag and drop UI experience is going to remain for quite some time and take a while before individuals adapt to chatting with AI systems.

Year to date performance

Adobe has been one of the shockers of the market this year as several SAAS companies have sold off on the assumption that AI will eventually replace their businesses.

Off all time high

From an all-time high perspective, Adobe has more than been cut in half from it's record high. Any time you get a crack at a premier software company more than -50% off, you need to take a hard look at the current and forward fundamentals.

One thing we notice here is that from a current and forward GAAP and Non-GAAP multiples, the numbers are pretty static, indicating a very tepid expected growth rate in the next 12 months to come. Some analysts remain more optimistic about growth rates than the numbers on screeners would lead you to believe. I am more optimistic as well.

Numbers don't lie

If we use reflexive patterns and simply compare the 10 year growth rates in earnings per share [EPS], free cash flow and revenue to price, they are now all in excess of the price appreciation over this period. Very bullish from a value perspective if any kind of forward growth is left in the tank in years to come. This chart indicates investors are betting on a contraction in those growth rates.

Historical GAAP P/E ratios

Where do we sit in respect to history on a GAAP P/E ratio basis? At a trailing 20.07 X, we can visually see this is a level not seen since the last big financial crisis circa 2012.

Adobe offerings and revenue exposure

Adobe is not sitting on it's hands when it comes to AI. They are proactively teaming up with LLM partners to improve all of their platforms. We can see from the above that ecosystem partners include both Google and OpenAI. It's true in my opinion that there will only be a handful of companies that can create useful LLMs due to having the capital to own the cloud computing data center infrastructure. We can already see they are leveraging that power more as partners than as adversaries to existing SAAS platforms.

In Adobe's most recent presentation they also mention plug ins in beta for ChatGPT, Copilot, TikTok, Linkedin and others. If you try to replicate some Adobe functions in an LLM like ChatGPT, the result is often clunky and nowhere near as good as what you could get on Adobe. Some of these LLMs may find it useful to simply cut to the front of the line, design a partnered plug in and now allow users to use these functions through the LLMs and be part of your LLM subscription. SAAS companies in short can also help LLMs improve and if they so choose to form these partnerships, then it would be very difficult from a legal perspective to make a full on competitive product in the future for an AI LLM company.

Adobe is still showing Y/Y growth in subscriber revenue despite the negative outlook by the market. While you could say they are just milking the existing user base with new add-ons, Adobe is also reporting a 25% growth in monthly active users [up to 700 million plus]. Additionally, they are reporting 30% growth Q/Q for their AI app Firefly. Firefly is the text to image and image generation feature similar to what ChatGPT's Dall E3 would do or Mid Journey.

So again, using Firefly as an example, they may be using ChatGPT as an engine but through their own custom user interface that users are already used to, hence growth on Adobe's platform remains even though you could go out and piece together similar functions elsewhere.

Fair price on a PEG ratio basis

Here I'll take a look at a PEG ratio analysis using bullish high end estimates for next year [26] and ending FY 2025 on the average EPS estimate of $20.8 as our starting point. The PEG ratio again divides the multiple by the growth rate less the percent sign. A ratio of 1 or less is a heck of a deal. We can pin down this price by taking the predicted growth rate as the multiplier times the predicted EPS as the multiplicand.

Here are the assumptions:

  • FY 2025: $20.8
  • FY 2026: $24.28
  • Growth rate : 16.73%
  • PEG 1 equivalent : 16.73 X $24.28 = $414.9
  • Selling at 78% of intrinsic value as of 12/2/2025

Let's see how this compares to Wall St. Ratings:

Again, this target is not wild or out to left field and actually sits below the average target for Wall St. analysts. I believe a price on Adobe in the low to mid $400s sits well with in the realm of logic based on the numbers.

Risks

The very risk that Wall St. is anticipating is the main risk. LLMs reduce their costs enormously and it becomes feasible to develop their own SAAS apps to directly compete with all others. If you combine this with an FTC that looks the other way for a decade or more and allows monopolies to form, this could be a big problem.

Adobe needs to make the market believe, or the LLMs need to make their goals clear

As of now, LLM companies are functioning more as synergistic partners than an enemy of all. If these companies made their stance and goals clear, this would go a long way to help Adobe and other SAAS companies like them.

I think the stock is cheap and a buy. The more I see partnerships with LLMs and continued growth, the more I think this will be an accelerator for great SASS companies rather than a huge problem.

data.title: Two Powerfully Bullish Developments
data.description:
Yesterday, stocks bounced back alongside the cryptocurrency space with Bitcoin regaining the $90,000 level. After six days of gains in the past seven for the S&P 500, I would not be surprised to see some consolidation in the coming days until we move past next week’s rate cut and enter the second half of December. Still, there is likely to be more rotation and positioning in sectors that are poised to rebound in the new year.

Investors may be concerned about abysmal consumer sentiment, bubbles in AI, monetary policy uncertainty, and the constant changes in fiscal policy, but the overriding factor to all of this is that earnings estimates continue to increase. Stock prices track earnings very closely over the long term, and inflection points in a bull market’s trend usually coincide with negative earnings revisions. We don’t have that today. In fact, earnings growth has been accelerating since mid-2023 to 12.9% in the third quarter. According to FactSet, the consensus expectation is for growth of 14.2% in 2026.

I have been noting the improvement in market breadth over the past month, as more stocks in sectors other than technology start to participate in this bull market. That establishes a more solid foundation from which we can see more bull market gains. I love to see technical developments in the market that support my fundamental outlook for the markets and economy. I have two to share today. The Zweig Breadth Thrust is a technical indicator development by famed investor Marty Zweig. It measures the number of advancing stocks on the exchange divided by the total number of advancers and decliners. It then generates a 10-day moving average of this percentage. A “buy” signal is triggered when this moving average rises from below 40% to above 61.5% within a 10-trading day period. Last week, it rose above 59%. While not a buy signal, the strength of the recovery over the past ten days has bullish implications.

In the prior 15 occurrences of rising to 59%, the S&P 500 was higher one year later 14 times with the only loss being 0.1% in 1974. That is a success rate of 93%. The good news doesn’t stop there as the S&P 500 has now risen seven months in a row, which is a rare feat that might suggest we are due for a more significant pullback. Perhaps, but this has happened 16 times since 1950, and the S&P 500 was higher seven months later every single time.

While there are no guarantees we will see a repeat performance, these bullish technical developments embolden my fundamental outlook for an eventual soft landing for the US economy, as the disinflationary trend reasserts itself, and the Federal Reserve moves closer to a neutral rate of interest. Consumer sentiment has likely bottomed, and a recovery will be another important tailwind for investors. I remain on offense and I am looking to position for the broadening in outperformance across various sectors of the market.

data.title: Topgolf Callaway: Leonard Green's Takeover Signals A New Chapter For The Entertainment Golf Giant
data.description:
If you're a shareholder of Topgolf Callaway Brands (MODG), you probably felt like you have been stuck in a sand trap for a while.

It has been quite a journey with this stock. After my 'Buy' article in March 2025, it went on to more than double. After the run-up, I updated my view in September to a 'Hold', because there was no promising news, and I wanted to flag some potential headwinds.

The price stabilized around $9.50 for a couple of months, until the Leonard Green announcement in late November sparked the next leg up, pushing shares toward $13.

MODG in 2025

If we look at the big picture after March, the stock was performing well and according to my expectations. But for me this ride has also been kind of frustrating and confusing.

The whole year, Topgolf Callaway company was publishing news about the potential separation of Topgolf and Callaway into two different entities, but for some reason they couldn't realize it for a really long time. In my opinion, the stock was cheap because of the uncertainty around Callaway and Topgolf Companies. In my view, the market was struggling to put a price tag on Topgolf, so the separation would be a clear valuation opportunity for both companies. So, I thought after the separation the stock price would go up. Then suddenly the CEO left and the new CEO had other plans, where he needed more time.

Then, last week, the news arrived.

The news that private equity firm Leonard Green & Partners is leading a deal to take a significant minority stake in Topgolf itself is more than just a financial transaction. It's a signal, a validation, and frankly, a potential catalyst this stock desperately needed.

As a longtime follower and investor in the company, I see this not as a simple headline, but as the beginning of a new chapter. And I believe it makes MODG a compelling buy. Let me explain why.

The Deal in Plain English: What's Actually Happening?

First, let's sum up the deal: Topgolf Callaway Brands is the parent company. It owns two entitites:

  1. The classic Callaway golf equipment business (clubs, balls, apparel).

  2. The Topgolf business (those high-tech driving ranges you see in the suburbs).

Leonard Green isn't buying the whole company. Instead, they are buying a 60% stake in its Topgolf and Toptracer business. Toptracer is the ball-tracking technology that makes Topgolf possible.

The deal values the Topgolf business at $1.1 billion. Here Callaway will maintain a 40% minority stake in the business, but since the deal is being finalized at a valuation of $1.1 billion, it is a 45% decline from Callaway's 2020 purchase price.

Why Didn't Topgolf Perform as Expected?

It's important to remember this was not the idea, that Callaway management originally had in 2020. The acquisition of Topgolf was a bet on a fundamentally different kind of growth engine.

Topgolf was considered the future of sports entertainment, and the company saw a big potential in this business model. As you can see, the shares were around $40 after Callaway bought the Topgolf Company. But later it did not go according to the plan.

The Business Model of Topgolf

Callaway wasn't just buying a driving range, it was investing in a sports entertainment phenomenon. Combining sports and entertainment wasn't a new thing; bowling alleys have been doing that for the last couple of decades. Topgolf did two things really well. They created a really good menu with good and tasty food combined with quality cocktails and drinks.

This is the first important point that differentiated Topgolf from traditional sports entertainment venues. The other genius was in targeting everyone possible. The serious golfer, the first-date couple, the office party, the family with kids. Another big factor for Topgolf was that golf (similar to tennis) was always considered a rich people's hobby. But Topgolf is social, and it is a tech-driven experience that only requires an hour or two. This demolished the traditional barriers of a 3-4 hour round of expensive golf, plus you could also play in bad weather.

With Topgolf, Golf became much more popular and more people from different demographics got the chance to play golf, first time in their lives.

The Vision

But the vision wasn't just about crowds; it was also about economics, and the business model was brilliant.

Topgolf strategically built the venues in wealthy, populous metropolitan areas. The initial CapEx was $20 to $35 million per venue (this depends on the size of the venue) for construction; the revenue machine was a well-distributed beauty.

If you have ever visited Topgolf, think about your own last visit.

The bay rental was not the only thing in the bill. Some certain part of it was in the burgers, the beers, and the corporate events. Only a third of Topgolf sales is coming from gameplay; the average customer spends quite a significant amount of money.

This model transformed the whole sports entertainment world, and new venues started getting founded every month. We started seeing Topgolf of Soccer, Topgolf of Baseball, and similar kinds of venues, and the whole industry grew like crazy.

So the business model was good. A scalable, weatherproof, high-margin entertainment business with a proven playbook for rapid expansion. It was a vision of a future where the golf ecosystem wasn't just about equipment, but about unforgettable experiences.

What Went Wrong?

Post-Covid Tailwinds

For a moment, it seemed like the stars had aligned. After Covid, the world was experiencing a collective entertainment phenomenon. The entertainment sector wasn't just recovering, it was exploding.

After being confined to their homes for nearly two years, people weren't just looking for a night out. They were craving their social, and shared experiences. The "revenge" mentality extended to local entertainment; there was a powerful urge to be out among people, to do something that felt new and exciting.

This created a perfect tailwind for the Topgolf. It was like the antithesis of lockdown life. High-energy, technology-driven social venue where you could finally gather with friends, celebrate, and be entertained. The company, with its expanding footprint of massive, nice, and clean venues, was uniquely positioned to capture this tidal wave of pent-up demand. It wasn't just selling golf, it was selling the very social connection people had been missing.

Correction of the Urges

But after the dust settled down, there were a couple of problems. First of all, in small markets, people were not revisiting the venues as many times as anticipated. For most of the people, it was an expensive experience, and same-store sales were declining in small markets.

Another problem was that the land leasings were long and expensive leases, which were hard to get out of. Considering the capital expenditure (CapEx) was also high, some of the small market venues were tied down with high rent and not guaranteed visitors for the next 10-20 years.

Another issue was that Topgolf started not being unique. Too many competitors were founded in the last 5 years. Even in Las Vegas (which is the highest-grossing Topgolf Venue) new venues with the same or similar setups started their own businesses, like Atomic Golf, Swingers Club (yeah, I know, what a name), etc. In the end there was way too much unclearness for the investors, and stock started declining.

Why The Leonard Green Takeover Is a Game-Changer for MODG Shareholders

I think this takeover is good for Callaway, Topgolf and also for Leonard Green. In my view this move is bullish for three key reasons:

1. It Establishes a "Floor Value"

For years, a core bull (or bear) thesis has been that the market wasn't properly valuing the explosive potential of Topgolf. The debt and complexity obscured the true value.

This deal slaps a certain ($1.1 billion) price tag on Topgolf. When you add that to the value of the Callaway brand, you quickly get a valuation that suggests the parent company is significantly undervalued at its current ~$2.3-2.4 billion market cap.

On the other side, Leonard Green is a respected firm with a long history of successful investments. They didn't just take a flyer; they did the due diligence and put a real number on the table. This provides a concrete benchmark that the public market had failed to establish.

2. It Fortifies the Balance Sheet (The Most Important Part)

This isn't just theoretical value; it is cash for Callaway. The deal is expected to inject approximately $770 million of primary capital into the Callaway.

It means Topgolf's aggressive, growth-fueled expansion plans can now be funded without affecting the parent company's balance sheet. It means the massive debt load, which has been a dark cloud over the stock, can be paid down significantly.

A stronger balance sheet reduces risk, lowers interest costs, and makes the entire enterprise more resilient. This directly addresses the single biggest worry for most shareholders.

3. It's a Powerful Vote of Confidence and Unlocks Strategic Flexibility

Leonard Green isn't just a passive investor. They are bringing expertise in scaling consumer and retail businesses. Their presence on the Topgolf board will provide strategic guidance and operational discipline. This partnership validates the global growth story of Topgolf and should help accelerate its path to profitability.

Leonard Green has the expertise and resources to finally take Topgolf truly global. Honestly, Topgolf's current international strategy seems like a missed opportunity to me. While they have international locations in Dubai and Bangkok, for some reason I have never understood, they have largely avoided major global hubs where golf and entertainment are hugely popular. I mean cities like Paris, Tokyo, Hong Kong, Macau, Istanbul, or Sydney.

Instead, in some regions they have chosen surprisingly out of the way spots. For example, their German venue is far from any major city. Similarly, in the United States, they expanded into higher-risk markets like Oklahoma before saturating safer ones. Leonard Green could refocus this strategy and unlock the brand's worldwide potential.

Furthermore, it creates strategic optionality. While an outright spin-off of Topgolf isn't on the table today, this move makes it a more plausible future lever to pull if it would create more shareholder value.

The Buy Case: Why I'm Adding to My Position

The market can finally start to focus on what makes this company special:

  • A Durable Golf Ecosystem: From buying a Callaway driver, to wearing Callaway apparel, MODG has built a powerful golf ecosystem that captures the modern golfer at every touchpoint.

  • Topgolf's Long Runway: The domestic and international expansion story for Topgolf is far from over. This deal ensures they have the fuel to build it.

  • A Re-Rating Opportunity: If the company demonstrates improved profitability and a cleaner balance sheet over the next few quarters, I believe the stock multiple will expand. The market hates uncertainty, and this deal surgically removes a huge chunk of it.

Financials of MODG

When it comes to financials, there's still work to do for Topgolf Callaway, but Leonard Green's move could turn things around quickly. The good news is we saw some green shoots in the last quarter. For Q3, core business revenue was up 3%, and the company beat its forecasts for both net revenue and adjusted EBITDA. Even more promising, after a tough period, Topgolf locations saw positive same-store sales growth again.

But I have to address the bottom line. A $14.7 million net loss for the first three quarters of 2025. Also, revenue tells an interesting story; almost half of the $934 million total came from Topgolf alone.

Here is the big question: because Callaway now owns less of Topgolf, that revenue line will shrink. The upside I see is Topgolf is also the main driver of operational costs. Considering this will be mostly Leonard Green's problem next year, 2026 could be the year profits return.

Looking at the rest of the business, the fundamentals are looking promising to me. The golf equipment industry is growing strongly worldwide (just look at Acushnet's stock), and brands like Jack Wolfskin in the "active lifestyle" category provide steady support.

For perspective, let's look at Acushnet (GOLF). They are almost a pure play on equipment and golf lifestyle, and they did about $2.46 billion in revenue last year, and the market values them at around $5 billion (a 22.75 forward P/E).

If we apply that same industry logic (and take 22.75 P/E as the industry benchmark) to Callaway's larger revenue base post-turnaround, the stock's potential upside becomes compelling.

The Risks

Of course, no investment is a sure thing. The consumer environment remains tricky. If a recession hits, not only could discretionary spending at places like Topgolf slow, people also would not spend money on new driver sets.

Execution risk on new venue openings always exists. And integrating a major new investor brings its own complexities. But these risks now feel manageable against the transformed financial backdrop.

Of course, the safest move would be to wait and see how Leonard Green's strategy plays out by mid-2026. But you might sit on the sidelines while the stock makes its biggest moves, as investors start betting on the turnaround well before the final results are in.

The Final Putt

The Leonard Green deal is the catalyst we all have been waiting for. It's not a magic wand that will erase all uncertainties, but it's an important move that strengthens Callaway's foundation, validates its crown jewel asset, and sets a clear path toward deleveraging and growth.

For too long, MODG has been an unclear future stock. The frustration of the past year has created a compelling entry point, and this news is the spark that should light the fire.

I see MODG as a "Buy" and believe shareholders who have held on through the rough patch are about to be rewarded.

data.title: Rocket Lab: This Could Be One Of The Biggest Moats In A Decade
data.description:
The Rocket Lab Investment Thesis

I think Rocket Lab Corporation (RKLB) is underestimated, as many overlook how the company is building a unique ecosystem to dominate the market in 5 to 10 years. If Rocket Lab is successful, this will be one of the most difficult moats to break. Therefore, I see Rocket Lab as undervalued in the long term.

Why do I think the moat could be huge?

In English, the expressions "it's not brain surgery" and "it's not rocket science" are used to express that something is not too difficult to understand. And "rocket science" is a synonym for something very difficult. And in fact it really is difficult. In order to launch a rocket into space, decades of research and testing are necessary to ensure that no accidents occur. Even with enormous financial resources, it is not possible to avoid years of testing. This means that companies that are already capable of launching and safely landing rockets have a huge knowledge advantage.

These companies also have a trust advantage, as their customers know that they deliver what they promise. This is probably why the Neutron start is delayed, as everything is supposed to run smoothly here. In my opinion, a failed launch would have much more serious consequences than a delay of a few months. In five years, no one will remember if the launch was delayed by a few months. However, if something serious goes wrong, it would put the company in trouble.

Once the launch is successful, the knowledge and trust advantages will ensure that customers have found a partner from whom they will find it difficult to part. Especially the trust advantage is often overlooked, despite being of crucial importance in government settings.

Rocket Lab and the government sector

I think satellites and rockets will play an increasingly important role in national security and intelligence. Missile defense and tracking of other threats will probably become much more important in the future. And Rocket Lab's focus on the lucrative government sector is also reflected in the backlog, which consists of 57% government orders.

Rocket Lab is not only trying to be mission critical through its satellites, but also through its solar cells. This is a clever move in my opinion, because such small parts are often very important. And if a company works precisely here, it strengthens its competitive advantage. The idea to acquire Mynaric, one of the pain points in the supply chain, is also very forward-looking, as it secures components that are in high demand. If Germany approves this purchase, Rocket Lab will also take a significant step towards European government contracts and acquire a crown jewel in the satellite industry.

Being able to quickly build and deploy entire satellite systems is the cornerstone of future U.S. defense strategy, and with this acquisition, we’re accelerating the delivery of mission critical payloads that support U.S. national security projects. This acquisition strengthens our role in building the resilient, responsive space architecture envisioned under Golden Dome, combining Geost’s proven sensing technologies with Rocket Lab’s ability to scale fast, secure, and integrated space solutions.

Source: Rocket Lab Website

The Geost acquisition is another step towards Rocket Lab's goal of producing satellites entirely in-house. By acquiring companies that are currently crucial for production, Rocket Lab is steadily building its competitive advantage.

Rocket Lab's platform system

Our spacecraft are highly customizable and entirely new platforms can be developed based on heritage hardware to meet our customers’ mission requirements.

Source: Rocket Lab Website

The Explorer platform from Rocket Lab, which allows for custom solutions and customer-specific options, could also be one of the deciding factors in the long term. This is particularly relevant, as different requirements may exist depending on the mission. And since Rocket Lab tries to do everything in-house, they can likely respond faster and more cost-effectively than the competition.

Depending on how the market develops, it will also be interesting to see where the best margins end up, and which area generates them. Will it be small, medium or large rockets? And in which area will the companies specialize in the future? I think Rocket Lab's strategy of doing everything in-house will enable them to react fast to whichever area they ultimately deem to be the most profitable.

Rocket Labs Space Software

In my opinion, the management team at Rocket Lab has a long-term outlook rather than a short-term mindset focused on quarterly results, as is often the case on Wall Street nowadays. Therefore, I believe they are also aware of how important software is to a good ecosystem. So Rocket Lab's space software could become a deciding factor in the future.

Real-time decision-making, simulations, flight software, and visualization offer endless opportunities to stand out from the competition. But, to be fair, with SpaceX (SPACE) and Blue Origin (BORGN), Rocket Lab is against two competitors who have a lot of experience in the ecosystem field. With Amazon (AMZN) and Tesla (TSLA), the founders of Blue Origin and SpaceX have gained a great deal of expertise in designing ecosystems. And both were very successful in the past. So personally, I wouldn't be surprised if the best software ultimately determines which company gets the most orders.

Another factor that could give Rocket Lab a small edge is its focus on small, reusable rockets.

The cash burn and the balance sheet

A key point for rapidly growing, unprofitable companies is always the cash burn rate. Rocket Lab's Net Income TTM was nearly -$200 million. But, I think the burn rate has not yet reached its peak. However, with a balance sheet of almost $1 billion in cash and ST investments, the company is in a secure spot for a few years.

But I still assume that the shareholders will be diluted further in the future and that there will be more stock placements.

Valuation

This message from the investor presentation clearly shows that the increase from three to four launches, together with the satellites, led to a significant increase in revenue. How high will the revenue growth be if the launches increase even faster? The upside is huge. It wouldn't surprise me if growth begins to accelerate in 2027+.

I think Rocket Lab could break the $2 billion revenue threshold in FY28, which would mean that Rocket Lab is currently trading at 10x FY28 revenue. It's expensive, but not completely overvalued for a company that will probably grow by 40% or more over the next few years.

Depending on how the satellite business develops and what M&A activities Rocket Lab carries out, the $2 billion revenue estimate could be even too low. The longer the time period under consideration, the more favorable the current valuation will be, provided that Rocket Lab continues to grow rapidly.

In my view, Rocket Lab is undoubtedly a high-risk, long-term investment. However, if Neutron is successful, it will generate substantial revenue. As a result, I see Rocket Lab as attractively valued given its potential and vision. So I will build a small position and hold it for ten years.

Risks

Further delays could pose a risk to the stock price, but only in the short term. In the long term, the driver will be whether Rocket Labs' offerings are reliable. It will also be important to consider how much the launches cost when they are performed more frequently. Will the economies of scale be noticeable quickly, or will it take a few years?

In general, I find Rocket Lab very interesting with its vision for the future, but it's difficult to estimate the exact business numbers at this point. It's more of a bet on the management team and their ability to build a huge moat.

Conclusion

Rocket Lab could be one of those companies that seem expensive at first glance, but are actually cheaply valued due to their enormous growth and TAM. Of course, Rocket Lab will probably experience drawdowns of 50% or more. But the company is very well positioned for the future.

The platform system, the reusable rockets, the focus on in-house production and the acquisition of companies that are a pain point in the production process all demonstrate an impressive level of foresight and that an incredibly strong moat could be created.

In the semiconductor sector, ASML Holding (ASML) identified Zeiss and Trumpf as key companies in the production chain a few years ago. To gain a competitive advantage, ASML purchased stakes in these companies. In my opinion, Rocket Lab's M&A activities aim for the same goal, ensuring that no one but them gets these high-quality, mission-critical small components. The Mynaric acquisition, for example, will enable Rocket Lab to offer its satellites on a large scale, as the company will have access to high-quality connectivity solutions. Currently, this is one of the weak points of the supply chain.

On the one hand, Rocket Lab secures important components for itself, and on the other hand, the competition has a hard time accessing these components. So the company strengthens its own production and weakens that of its competitors. The ASML playbook is therefore implemented perfectly here.

Additionally, the Nvidia (NVDA) playbook is being applied, as Nvidia currently has a strong competitive advantage with CUDA. Rocket Labs' focus on software will therefore enable them to build a moat that is difficult to break through. The better the software is calibrated, the more accurately the satellites can be placed in space. This is particularly important because Rocket Labs aims to be fully vertically integrated, setting them apart from the competition.

Furthermore, many competitors do not offer launch services. This is already a competitive advantage, which will likely become even stronger in the future. Rocket Lab seems to be pursuing the goal of offering everything in-house. If the company achieves this goal, Rocket Lab will have lower production costs and much more flexibility to offer customized solutions to customers. This would set them apart from other companies in the industry that focus only on specific areas, such as launch services or satellites. So if Rocket Lab can successfully handle everything in-house, they could establish a significant moat.

So ultimately, I would describe Rocket Lab as a high risk, high reward long-term play.

data.title: Federal Realty: This 58-Year Dividend Growth REIT Looks Ready To Run
data.description:
During the pandemic, we invested in two retail REITs as part of our Retirement Portfolio.

Back then, the entire sector was heavily discounted, so we picked the bluest blue chips that we could find.

Those were Regency Centers (REG) and Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT).

Since then, REG has strongly recovered, which led us to sell our position in late 2024 for a 121.8% total return over our ~4-year holding period.

FRT, on the other hand, has yet to fully recover. It has earned us a near 60% total return since we first bought it in May of 2020, which isn't bad, considering the bear market we have suffered since then.

But it is far short of what REG earned us, despite both being blue-chip retail REITs. This suggests that FRT might have more upside to offer in the coming years.

Here is our updated investment thesis:

Federal Realty Investment Trust: The Best of Best Retail Real Estate

Most REITs focus on Class A properties.

But even within the Class A segment, there are vast differences in property and location quality.

FRT arguably owns the best retail real estate portfolio in the nation.

And that's not just my potentially biased opinion. It is backed by real data.

First, let's look at their locations. After all, the long-term success of real estate investments typically comes down to location, location, location... and FRT is miles apart from its peers from this perspective. Its properties are located in the most affluent and densely populated neighborhoods of the country:

This means that its properties enjoy the greatest barriers to entry. These locations are already fully built out, have strict zoning, and have high replacement costs, and they are competing with better-use redevelopment projects that could even reduce retail space over time, making FRT's portfolio truly irreplaceable.

The higher household incomes also mean that its properties should enjoy greater stability across cycles, as more affluent consumers are typically less impacted by recessions and retain much greater purchasing power.

This theory was proven during the Great Financial Crisis, when FRT was the only retail REIT to keep steadily growing its cash flow even as most of its peers faced significant pain.

Federal Realty Investment Trust

But it is not just the superior locations of its properties that set FRT apart.

The properties themselves also stand out. FRT differentiates itself by typically targeting larger open-air properties, often 250,000 square feet or bigger, with lots of acreage. These are not just your typical neighborhood centers that pull traffic from a 3-mile radius. Rather, they are dominant regional centers that can pull consumers from a 10, 15, or even 20-mile radius. They are important regional marketplaces that retailers need to be involved with, resulting in significant demand, despite the limited supply of such properties, especially in these locations.

The large size, significant acreages, and superior locations also give FRT an opportunity to turn these into mixed-use destinations with significant non-retail components. This explains why the REIT today generates about 10% of its rental income from apartment units and another 10% from mixed-use office.

Here are a few examples from its portfolio to give you an idea.

Santana Row

Assembly Row

Pike & Rose

My final point on FRT's superior asset quality is that it has allowed the REIT to hike its dividend for 58 years in a row. This is the longest dividend growth streak in the entire REIT sector. There is no better proof than this to demonstrate that FRT owns true trophy assets that are strictly limited in supply but steadily growing in demand.

And now comes the interesting part for investors:

Historically, the market has rightfully priced this superior quality and performance with a premium valuation relative to its close peers.

This is well-justified given that its properties are structurally superior, and this should result in superior performance over the long run.

It also doesn't hurt that the REIT has a BBB+ rated balance sheet and its management has one of the best track records in the entire REIT sector. Since the current CEO took over 22 years ago, the company has delivered significant outperformance relative to its peers.

But despite all of these qualities that justify a premium valuation, the REIT is now priced right in line with its peer group. They have lost their premium, and the management just recently discussed this at an investor conference. Here is what they said:

"We've increased the dividend to our shareholders every year since 1967. There's not another REIT that can say that. It is a really high-quality portfolio. It is one that has done better than most certainly over that very long period of time. But in the last few years, as multiples have contracted, as interest rates have gone up, as we dealt with the overhang from COVID, we wind up trading very similar to other companies."

I think that this is because of two main reasons.

First, as I noted earlier, the REIT generates 10% of its rental income from office space, which has been out of favor ever since the pandemic. I agree with the market that the office sector is quite undesirable. But these are not your average office buildings. They themselves call them "mixed-use offices" because they are located in communities where the retail is the reason people gather at the site. They are in fully amenitized environments that create a lot of value for the office user, and that explains why they enjoy a 96% occupancy rate, even as the average vacancy rate of the office sector is over 20%. The best of the best office properties have so far benefited from the transition to hybrid work, as tenants have consolidated their office footprint towards higher-quality properties with superior amenities that would motivate their employees to return to the office. This is exactly what FRT owns.

Second, the REIT is perceived to be somewhat more cyclical than many of its peers, which focus primarily on grocery-anchored properties. FRT is more exposed to traditional retail that relies on discretionary spending. This seems to concern the market a bit as we go through uncertain times, and it may explain why FRT has lost its premium valuation.

But as we noted earlier, the past performance of its properties, through various recessions and black swan events, shows that they are at least as resilient, if not more, than those of its peers.

The reality is that when you own the best of the best assets in your category and the supply of such assets is strictly limited, you are the one who holds the cards. Retailers are fighting to get space in these properties, and when they get the chance to sign a 10-year lease, they are making long-term decisions, understanding that there will be downcycles along the way. They are not going to forgo the ability to be in that center over a temporary slowdown.

So, in short, I think that FRT deserves to trade at a premium to its peers, but it trades right in line due to reasons I believe are unjustified, and that is why FRT remains attractive today.

And the future looks bright.

Retail is today severely undersupplied, with some studies showing that retail rents would need to rise by up to 65% to make new development projects economically feasible.

Therefore, good retail properties are likely to keep enjoying 3-5% annual same-property NOI growth for many years to come. That is right around what FRT has guided for this year and also next year.

When a certain property sector is undersupplied, it is always a good idea to own the best assets in that sector, as they should be in the strongest position to push rents higher.

Beyond organic growth, FRT has historically been very successful at driving additional external growth by selling mature assets with limited upside at premium valuations in order to reinvest in new properties with greater value-add potential to drive long-term growth.

Their large scale, relationships, and expertise afford a significant competitive advantage in this space, especially as there is more limited competition to purchase these bigger retail assets than the smaller grocery-anchored properties that most of its peers are targeting. This provides FRT a greater ability to create value and earn higher yields post-improvements.

They currently also have about $1.5 billion worth of development projects underway, which is significant relative to their $8.6 billion market cap. Most of these projects have already been funded and will start to contribute significant additional cash flow over the coming years, with limited additional spend required:

Finally, they have significant future additional densification opportunities in their portfolio to build additional residential units and commercial space.

We noted earlier that these are large properties with significant acreages in densely populated locations with affluent demographics. This affords FRT superior redevelopment opportunities when compared to its peers:

In our Kimco (KIM) investment thesis, I also discussed how the long-term growth of autonomous vehicles could significantly reduce the need for large parking spots at such retail properties, offering an opportunity for the REITs to redevelop portions of this valuable land for better use.

Few REITs would benefit more from that long-term scenario than FRT, given how valuable its land is.

It currently trades at 13.5x FFO, roughly in line with its peer group, but as its growth accelerates in the coming years due to the improvements in retail fundamentals, even as interest rates are cut as well, we expect it to eventually regain a premium multiple.

This could unlock 20-30% upside from here, but even ignoring this potential upside, FRT should be in a position to drive high-single-digit or low-double-digit total returns from its 4.6% dividend yield and steady long-term growth.

Combining the yield, growth, and some upside from repricing, we expect 12-15% annual total returns over the coming years, which makes FRT a great anchor position for our more conservative Retirement Portfolio.

We maintain our Buy rating and have no plans to sell our position.

data.title: AMLP Provides Access To Midstream Standouts Exceeding An 8% Yield
data.description:
I have been a fan of Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) for a long time as it's been an indirect play to gain exposure to the midstream sector. The last decade has been a tug of war between yield and risk as traditional income strategies such as treasuries and high-grade corporate bonds have been volatile due to fluctuations in interest rates. Some high-dividend equities have seen their multiples expand significantly which has caused the current yields to compress for new investors looking to build out an income strategy. Energy infrastructure has remained very interesting as the midstream sector is reemerging as one of the more compelling income opportunities available in todays market.

The Alerian MLP ETF is designed to be a simple way to own the basket of master limited partnerships ((MLPs)) through it's ETF for large amounts of recurring income without the K-1 implications that MLPs are known for. AMLP tracks the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index which is a basket of MLPs that earn the majority of their cash flow from activities like transporting, storing, and processing energy commodities. In an environment where data center buildouts are on the rise, these are the tollbooths for the traditional energy system as these assets generate revenue based on volumes moving through their networks, not on the day-to-day price of oil or gas. I think there are many interesting names in the sector and AMLP is still an interesting ETF that I am bullish on and adding to as I see value in many of its underlying holdings.

Following up on my previous article

Back in July I wrote an article about AMLP where I discussed why I was still bullish on its future prospects. I believed that AMLP offered the ability for investors to gain exposure to America's energy infrastructure system which would benefit from surging energy demand driven by AI and data center expansion. I liked the protection that midstream operators provided because they have been traditionally insulated from commodity price swings via fee-based contracts that have provided stable cash flow and attractive amounts of income. Since June 16th when my last article was written (can be read here) shares of AMLP have declined by -4.01% while the S&P 500 rallied 13.95%. AMLP's total return over this period was 0.06% and I still believe that its current share price is providing the income community with an opportunity to lock in yields that are exceeding 8%. I am long AMLP and adding to my position.

Strong investment

The amount of income being generated by an asset is only as strong as the underlying cash flow and if the cash flow is fragile there is an increased chance that the amount of income being generated in the future will decline. One of AMLP's strengths is that its holdings are not speculative early stage companies as they are established midstream operators with hard assets that construct the backbone of the energy industry. MLPs such as Energy Transfer (ET), and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) own and operate pipelines, storage tanks, fractionation facilities, and processing plants that allow the American energy system to function. These assets are built to last decades and in many cases midstream MLPs are paid based on volumes moving through their systems under long-term "take-or-pay" or fee-based agreements. The structure reduces direct exposure to volatility in commodity pricing that can be impacted by OPEC or the pure supply and demand economics regarding oil. Their revenue is more closely tied to the overall demand for energy through heating, industrial usage, exports, and power generation for data centers and AI-driven cloud infrastructure. The distributions that AMLP is collecting from its underlying holdings are supported by tangible infrastructure that is difficult and expensive to replicate.

I believe that a big misconception around MLPs is that they are leveraged bets on oil and gas prices as many of its underlying holdings have the majority of their revenue tied to fee-based contracts. For instance EPD has 89% of their natural gas processing contracts tied to a fee component while 90% of ET's Adjusted EBITA is tied to fee-based contracts. The index AMLP tracks focuses on MLPs that earn at least 50% of their EBITDA from assets not directly exposed to commodity price swings which favors the type of contracts I am a big fan of. This also correlates to predictable cash flow for income investors that can be paid through the distribution. While commodity price cycles can affect volumes and the pace of new projects, the revenue model for many of AMLP's underlying holdings is closer to a utility or infrastructure business than a pure commodity producer.

The fact that AMLP doesn't issue a K-1 removes a large barrier to entry for many investors who are interested in MLPs. Please check with your accountant before making any decisions but in my experience MLPs have traditionally created a complexity come tax season as investors have to file in multiple states. Since the fund is taxed as a regular corporation investors hold traditional ETF shares and receive a standard Form 1099 instead of a K-1. A portion of the distributed income could also be classified as return of capital which can also be beneficial. AMLP becomes an investment vehicle that is a pure-play ticket toward midstream operators. AMLP provides diversified exposure to the midstream MLP universe which takes the guess work out of the picture. I think that taking an index methodology approach to this sector allows investors to own a large portion of America's energy infrastructure and generate diversified distributions that get paid put in a quarterly dividend.

K-1 Tax Package

The purpose of a K-1 form is for each partner to report their share of a business's gains, losses, and distributions to the government. MLPs issue a Schedule K-1 to its investors. To read more about Schedule K-1 please click here. AMLP is an ETF that provides exposure to MLPs that issue a K-1, but the actual ETF does not issue a K-1 to its shareholders. Please click here to read about the tax advantages of owning AMLP.

AMLP has become a superior income play compared to the risk-free rate of return

Over the past decade the risk-free rate of return hasn't been as lucrative as in recent years. The United States 12-Month Bill Yield (US12M) has gone from a 0.67% yield to over 5% back down to 3.59%. If you had purchased a $1,000 US12M bond a decade ago you would have generated $6.70 in income during year 1 when the yield was 0.67%. You would have got caught rolling it over during the pandemic and other years when rates were still under 2%. Ultimately you would have generated $6.70 of income in year 1, $8.60 in year 2, $16,70 in year 3, $26.80 in year 4, $15,60 in year 5, $0.86 in year 6, $2.60 in year 7, $47.10 in year 8, $41.80 in year 9, and $35.90 in year 10. This would have generated $202.66 over a decade which works out to an annualized yield of 2.03% annually. The yield on the United States 10-year (US10Y) a decade ago was 2.14% so by purchasing a 10-year note you would have done a little better by generating $214 of income on a $1,000 bond.

Shares of AMLP traded for $51.50 a decade ago so you could have purchased 19.42 shares. Unlike the US12M shares of AMLP have declined by -8.1% over the past decade but its income has made up for it. On an adjusted basis for the split that occurred each share of AMLP generated $37.20 of income. Your underlying investment of $1,000 would have lost -$80.97 and your shares at the current price would be worth $919.03. You would have generated $722.26 in income over the years and after the $80.97 is taken out of the income generated to make the investment whole you would be left with $641.29 in pure income. Just the pure income is a 64.13% yield or 6.41% annualized. Looking at this without taking the loss into consideration AMLP would have generated a yield on cost of 72.23% over the past decade or 7.22% annualized. AMLP has been a superior investment rather than staying on the sidelines in risk-free assets and generated significantly more income even after the share depreciation is accounted for.

Risks to investing in AMLP

While I am bullish on AMLP, there are several risks that investors should consider, and they should do their own due diligence before allocating capital toward AMLP. AMLP is heavily concentrated in the energy sector as it focuses on midstream operators, which makes the ETF highly sensitive to developments throughout the energy sector. Currently, we're facing an energy renaissance as data centers are driving the demand for energy higher. If the CapEx allocation toward data centers changes and we reach the point where the future buildout stalls the narrative around energy could be impacted and midstream operators could find themselves in a situation where people look for other opportunities in the market. Energy is also not an exciting name so investors are likely to do better with a standard S&P 500 index fund from a capital appreciation standpoint. Investors should be very cautious about AMLP and make sure they are allocating capital to it for the right reasons.

Conclusion

I'm currently adding to AMLP because it's not a generic dividend ETF as it's targeted at a niche of the market where cash flows are high, tangible, and tied to real assets that keep the economy running. I like the fact that a company can't raise capital and decide that they want to build a pipeline or storage facilities. There are high barriers to entry for energy infrastructure companies that favor the incumbents. I also like the fact that AMLP does not issue a K-1 but rather a 1099 which makes my life easy at the end of the year. I believe that AMLP's blend of high yield backed by hard assets is a big differentiator in an environment where the risk-free rate of return is declining. I'm bullish on AMLP as I believe it will continue to generate large amounts of yield while being in a position where the underlying share price can continue higher as more assets come online that increase the demand for energy across the country.

data.title: Private Credit Is Cracking: Are Public Equities Next?
data.description:
Over the last year, private credit has started to show several signs of stress, which made me ask the one question that matters (to investors in public equities): can a private credit blowout spill into public equities?

My answer is that it can, but the extent is quite limited. Right now, the damage seems to be contained in the private credit world. And, as I discuss in this article, I don’t see strong signs of any forced deleveraging that could mechanically tank the entire equity market. So far, the pessimism surrounding private credit is dragging down the main publicly traded alternative credit managers, but I don't see a spillover into other sectors like tech.

That said, since July 2024, there have been multiple sponsor-to-lender handovers, restructurings, and bankruptcies across very different assets, alongside renewed criticism that private marks can lag reality. In this article, I review the different events that led to rising pessimism in the private credit market. Buffett calls these factors cockroaches, so I’ll use a similar term below.

A Review Of The Cockroaches In Private Credit

There are a few patterns that, taken together, indicate a deterioration in the US private credit market.

The first cockroach in the kitchen was spotted on July 2, 2024, when Vista Equity was reportedly in talks to give up control of its company, Pluralsight, to private-credit lenders via a debt-to-equity conversion.

In my view, this marked the first major debt restructuring of a company that had borrowed from "shadow banks". To keep the terminology clear, on Wall Street, shadow banks are commonly known as lenders that don’t face the same regulatory obligations as traditional banks. More often than not, less regulation means more risks, and this is exactly what happened with Pluralsight. The red flag in the Vista story was the markdown to zero of a $1.6 billion equity investment in Pluralsight just a few months earlier (according to Reuters). Among the lenders that took control over the company, we have Blue Owl Capital, Ares Management, Golub Capital, Oaktree, Benefit Street Partners, Goldman Sachs, and BlackRock. After the restructuring was completed, a big chunk of debt ($1.2B according to some sources) was converted to equity.

As per Buffett , "there is seldom just one cockroach in the kitchen". Since the Pluralsight restructuring, I count 5 more. I discuss them briefly below.

In January 2025, Alacrity Solutions (owned by BlackRock) went through a similar restructuring where the sponsor relinquished control and direct lenders took ownership. Among those lenders, we have some familiar names again: Blue Owl, KKR, and Goldman Sachs AM. Roughly $1B senior and $0.5B junior debt converted into a $450M term loan plus about $250M preferred equity.

On February 6, 2025, Zips Car Wash (owned by Atlantic Street Capital) filed for Chapter 11. The company was seeking to cut $279M in debt and hand the keys to its lenders. Among the reasons for the bankruptcy, Zips cited rising interest rates, higher labor costs, and intensifying competition (all of which make sense to me, considering the tough business the company was operating in). The issue, however, was pointed out by Bloomberg, citing that loans were marked in the low-$0.90s relatively shortly before bankruptcy.

In other words, no one was able to see the train coming, and this fueled criticism that private marks can lag reality. Interestingly enough, the same criticism was made regarding the Alacrity Solutions restructuring.

Moving on, International Data Group (backed by Blackstone) was the next domino on the line to fall. Yet again, direct lenders converted part (20%-25%) of their debt (estimated at around $800M) into equity in April 2025.

The next two bankruptcies should be familiar to most readers due to their size and the fact that they happened within a short timeframe (which led to a lot of attention in the media). I'm talking about First Brands Group and Tricolor.

First Brands Group filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in September, with some irregularities detailed on September 30. The company said that it found an apparent $2.3B hole tied to off-balance-sheet invoice financing. The concerns included whether invoices were factored multiple times and whether collateral was commingled. According to the last filing, excluding factoring liabilities, the company had $9.3B of debt obligations. Another concern was related to a spillover to funds and banks. Jefferies disclosed $715M in receivables exposure via its asset-management unit, and UBS cited over $500M exposure in affected funds. On top of that, there is a DOJ inquiry and an SEC probe of Jefferies’ dealings. This bankruptcy added fuel to the fire in the private credit markets, especially as the bankruptcy was (likely) due to opacity and complexity in credit products.

At the same time, Tricolor filed for Chapter 7 liquidation. The size of this liquidation includes over $1B in assets and over $1B liabilities, with about 25,000 creditors. The controversy here came from one of the lenders, Fifth Third, which warned of alleged external fraudulent activity tied to a $200M loan. As a consequence, Fifth Third expects a $170M–$200M impairment, and JPMorgan had nearly $200M exposure (according to Reuters sources). I argue this was the icing on the cake, as it was a direct hit to the confidence in parts of asset-backed lending (remember that Tricolor was in the auto loan / used-car dealer business), adjacent to private credit.

Finally, and I wouldn't consider this a cockroach, we have the Blue Owl saga. As you may know from my article on Blue Owl, the company proposed merging its non-traded BDC (OBDC II) into its publicly traded BDC (OBDC). The problem came from a timing perspective, as in November, OBDC’s stock price was trading approximately 20% below its NAV. Here is the trade that shrewd investors quickly noticed:

Given that OBDC II investors could normally redeem at or near NAV, and the fact that OBDC shares were available in the market at a 20% discount, a good trade would be to redeem OBDC II at NAV, then buy OBDC cheaper in the market. Naturally, this kind of arbitrage would create a quick exit from OBDC II. To prevent such an avalanche, the company decided to stop OBDC II redemptions until the merger closed (expected Q1 2026). As a consequence, the company terminated the proposed merger on November 19, 2025. However, management said that they are planning to turn the tender window back on again in early 2026, so OBDC II investors would once again have a formal way to request cash out. I recommend keeping a close eye on any developments in the Blue Owl OBDC saga, as the story may not be over.

Am I Sweating?

Not really, but I am indeed keeping a close eye on any new cockroaches surfacing in private credit.

In my view, we are looking at a contained pain, specific to this sector. In other words, I don't see the cockroaches moving from the kitchen into the bedroom (i.e., hyperscalers and AI-exposed companies). I believe the spillover to broad public equities is likely limited. The IMF estimates that the most common private credit investment vehicle is a closed-end, capital call structure with a limited life, meaning investors generally can’t demand cash tomorrow. This leads to fewer forced panic-selloffs (remember 2008).

Speaking of 2008, banks are better capitalized, and according to the last FEDS report, "the financial stability implications of banks' lending to private credit seem limited so far". Furthermore, the Fed’s Financial Stability Report (released back in April this year) describes the banking system as sound and resilient. Note on the "leverage in the financial sector" column below that broker leverage is low.

Furthermore, the Fed also noted a split between public and private firms, with weaker coverage ratios being more of an issue for private firms.

My final argument is the fact that private credit is just 9% of the total outstanding nonfinancial corporate debt. At the moment, most of the headwinds are contained

Looking Ahead

The chart below shows the 1-year performance of publicly traded alternative credit managers vs the S&P500.

Given the deterioration in sentiment due to the factors that I discussed in this article, all of them have underperformed the S&P500 and are down double digits over the last 12 months.

From a fundamentals perspective, all these alternative credit managers have experienced decent revenue growth over the last 3-5 years. Furthermore, the 1-year top-line projections suggest double-digit growth for all these players, with KKR and Blue Owl projected to grow 24% yoy next year.

Call me crazy, but I don't see a clear justification for the underperformance of these stocks over the last year, except for the deterioration in sentiment.

Looking ahead, I would keep a close eye on the Blue Owl OBDC saga, and I would closely monitor more bankruptcies in the private and public sectors. From what I'm seeing, most of the cockroaches spotted since mid-2024 are not suggesting systemic risks that could spill to public equities. Most of them are the result of tough business models (used car sales) and complex credit products. I just don't see enough leverage in the private credit markets and a clear catalyst ahead that would suggest a blowout.

data.title: SCHD ETF: 2025 Reconstitution Impact And 2026 Outlook
data.description:
SCHD ETF: energy exposure in focus

I last covered the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) on 10.21 with an article titled “SCHD ETF: REIT Dividends Too Attractive To Exclude”. That article was motivated by the ETF’s last dividend declaration and gave it a Hold rating. Since that analysis, there have been a few notable changes worthy of a reexamination. In the rest of this article, I will concentrate on the top 2 changes on my list: the fund’s latest exposure disclosure and also the macroeconomic parameters facing oil prices.

As a background, the SCHD has long been a popular foundational holding for many income-focused investors and the fund performs an but its annual reconstitution (typically in March of every year). The 2025 reconstitution saw a notable increase in the ETF's exposure to the Energy sector (more on this later). Due to such a shift, the energy sector is now the fund’s largest exposure, representing about 19.3% of its total assets per the fund’s more recent disclosure shown below. Next, I will explain why such a shift has been detrimental to its 2025 total return and could continue to be so in 2026 due to the ongoing oil price pressure.

SCHD: basic information and exposure

Let me take a quick digression and cover the basics of SCHD in case there are readers new to the fund. As indicated in its name, SCHD focuses on dividend paying stocks. Its stock selection methodology is centered on assessing dividend quality, which relies on a framework of four distinct financial metrics as depicted in the chart and quoted from its fund description below:

SCHD Fund Description: The Fund’s goal is to track as closely as possible, before fees and expenses, the total return of the Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100™ Index. SCHD’s index focuses on the sustainability and quality of dividends by screening companies based on four dividend quality attributes. These screens have provided enhanced yield and quality factors to the portfolio, along with distinct sector exposures.

As a reflection (or a result) of its above approach, the fund’s holdings are heavily tilted towards more defensive sectors and stocks. The SCHD ETF holds a relatively small number of 102 stocks, with its Top 10 Holdings constituting a substantial 42.30% of the entire portfolio as you can see from the following table. The top holdings include several healthcare stocks with its largest single position being Merck & Co at 4.98%, followed closely by Amgen at 4.86%. Other top holdings include consumer staples (Coca-Cola Co at 4.24% and PepsiCo at 4.00%). Even though Chevron is the only energy stock in the top 10, the sector represents the fund’s largest exposure as aforementioned and I will elaborate on the implications immediately below.

SCHD ETF: 2025 annual reconstitution and 2026 outlook

The next two charts summarize the fund’s 2025 annual reconstitution performed in March. As seen in the first bar chart, the largest net additions are toward energy stocks, which saw an addition of 5 holdings. As seen in the second table below, these changes have resulted in a net exposure increase of 8.8% toward the energy sector, bringing the fund’s exposure to energy stocks to 21.0%. Due to the underperformance of energy stocks since then, the weight of these stocks has decreased to the 19.3% as aforementioned, implying a relative underperformance of around 9%.

Looking ahead, I am afraid SCHD’s heavy exposure to energy stocks will keep dragging its performance as I see persisting pressure on oil prices. Out of the many factors, three of them stand out in my mind. First, I expect OPEC+ countries to continue production at elevated levels for 2026 and likely for 2027 also (see this Seeking Alpha news for more background). Second, I see large odds for the Ukraine peace talks to lead to the end of this multi-year war, thus bringing back Russian, a key oil-exporting country, back to the global energy market.

The third and probably most crucial factor is China. Recent reports (e.g., see this Wall Street Journal report and also the EIT estimates quoted below) show that China kept filling its strategic petroleum reserves. The sheer magnitude of China’s demand translates into a strong supporting force for oil prices. This could be double-edged sword because oil prices could drop considerably if the country’s demand softens. As an example, the Wall Street Journal report mentioned above projects that “If China really stops buying, the path toward low $50 would be very quick”.
